title: A Case Study Of Chinese Netizens’ Opinions On Capital Punishment
citation: Jian Hong Liu, & Bin Liang, A Case Study of Chinese Netizens’ Opinions on Capital Punishment: Diversity, Rationale, and Interaction, 45(6), Modern China, 666 (2019).
abstract: Empirical studies of Chinese public opinion on capital punishment are very rare. Almost all past studies suffered from questionable survey designs and/ or nonrepresentative/nonrandom sampling (e.g., student samples). In this study, to examine the breadth and the depth of public opinion on capital punishment, we turn to Chinese netizens' opinions online on a particular capital case, the Lin Senhao poisoning case. Though our netizen sample has its own limitations, it is innovative and encompasses a broader group of representations than in past studies. Specifically, we address three questions: the diversity of netizens' opinions, the rationales for netizens' support for Lin's death sentence, and netizens' interactions. This study is the first to examine Chinese netizens' opinions in a capital case in detail and to uncover potential complexities and nuances of such opinions.
conclusion: This study is the very first to examine netizens’ opinions in depth in a famous capital case. Our analysis has focused on three issues: the diversity of netizen’s opinions, the rationales for netizens’ support for Lin’s death sentence, and the interactions among netizens. Despite the innovative nature and strength of our data, there are some limitations to our methodology. First, our sample could have suffered from selection biases. For instance, people who do not have access to the internet due to the digital divide may have held different opinions on capital punishment. Second, our data lacked individual and personal background information. One particular concern is the identity of the netizens whose online comments we analyzed. In addition, it is unknown to what extent governmental censorship might have played a role (e.g., through hiring official trolls to “clean” the net). Third, our analysis in this study was limited to only one murder case, and netizens’ opinions may be very different in other capital cases (e.g., corruption cases). Despite these limitations, a number of potential lessons can be drawn from netizens’ discussions. First, though most netizens (by our count) supported Lin’s death sentence, there is considerable variation in the breadth and the depth of netizens’ comments. Strikingly, there was both support and opposition to almost every subject discussed by netizens, from support for and justification of Lin’s death sentence, to sympathy for Lin, to views about Lin’s and Huang’s families, to views about Lin’s defense, and to issues about judicial verdicts. For instance, netizens explicitly called for the abolition of China’s death penalty in ninety-three comments, questioned the utility of capital punishment and the fairness of its use, and even argued that it is barbaric and a violation of human rights. Such bold voices and opposition show that the claim of “overwhelming public support” for the death penalty is likely misleading and fails to uncover the nuances of netizens’ opinions. Second, despite the diversity in opinions, the influence of Chinese culture on the opinions of the majority of netizens is apparent. Concepts such as sharen changming and killing one to deter a hundred heavily influenced netizens’ support for Lin’s death sentence. It is also interesting to see how a traditional sense of justice and fairness turned around against the victim’s family when Huang’s father was pondering a suit for civil compensation after Lin’s execution. To many netizens, going after the Lin family’s money was “greedy” and not “humane” once Lin had paid his debt with his own life. The influence of such traditional culture is what makes China “Chinese,” but may pose obstacles to further reforms of China’s use of capital punishment. For instance, many openly cast doubt on the prolonged but required judicial review process and called it “delaying and wasteful.” Their willingness to forgo legal procedures is troubling. Third, many netizens’ opinions also reflected their attitudes toward the existing system, from problems with the educational system (e.g., the failure of Lin’s education), to problems with the medical system (e.g., many questioned the failure of the hospital to save Huang’s life), to declining morality, to privileges enjoyed by government officials and the wealthy, and to the fairness of the judicial system. For instance, many of Lin’s supporters questioned his death sentence because they saw it as disproportionately harsh compared to other crimes/criminals (e.g., corrupt officials). Official corruption, in fact, is one of few issues that generated no disagreement among netizens, which may signal future difficulty in restricting the use of capital punishment in corruption crimes (see Miao, 2013). The lesson is that to change people’s opinions, more structural changes to the existing systems might be necessary. Lastly, an examination of netizens’ interactions shows both signs of irrationality (e.g., use of foul language and personal attacks) and rationality (e.g., putting oneself in others’ shoes, calling for rationality). In Furman v. Georgia, 408 U.S. 238 (1972), Justice Thurgood Marshall suggested that American citizens would be swayed by reasoned persuasion that included information about the practice of American capital punishment. Empirical tests of the Marshall hypothesis have primarily relied on a unidirectional intervention model in which information (intervention) is passed from the researcher to the subjects. In a rare effort, Sato (2014) turned to discussions among focus group participants and explored how public deliberation (two-way discussions) may change Japanese people’s support for capital punishment. Our study has examined netizens’ exchanges in a natural setting without any artificial intervention. The evidence shows that there was little compromise and adjustment, not to mention complete change, among emotionally charged netizens whose minds were often made up from the very beginning. Though there are obvious limitations with this form of online exchange (e.g., confusing or conflicting information, little guidance), the results raise the question of whether netizens’ exercise of freedom of speech online can bring positive changes to their opinions on capital punishment in China.
keywords: netizens’ opinions on capital punishment, Lin Senhao case, diversity of public opinion, rationales for capital punishment, netizens’ interactions

dataSourceIdentification: Sina.com.cn 新浪网
authorsDataDescription: The data utilized in this study are part of a larger project to explore and examine Chinese netizens’ opinions on the death penalty. Given the impracticability of conducting a national representative survey in China and other weaknesses associated with survey studies, we turned to an online comment forum from which we collected netizens’ comments on news about China’s death sentences. Specifically, data were collected in spring 2016 in two steps. First, we conducted an extensive news search via the search engine of Sina.com.cn 新浪网 (one of the largest and most popular commercial websites in China). All stories that reported death penalty cases in 2015 (from January 1 to December 31) were included in our sample. In cases with multiple defendants, as long as one of them was sentenced to death, the case was included. Second, once we identified all such cases, we collected all case reports and netizens’ comments that followed the reports and stored them separately as documents for analysis. Sina.com.cn creates a special zone for netizens’ comments for each piece of news, which serves exactly our purpose. Given our focus on netizens’ comments, we removed cases with fewer than three comments. A total of 64 cases with 121 defendants were collected. The crimes involved covered fourteen different offenses, led by homicide, drug trafficking, rape, and kidnapping (leading to human death). Given the impracticability of summarizing all cases in one research project, we focus on Lin’s case in this study. Among all the cases, Lin’s generated the most comments (n = 17,611). Specifically, Lin’s case was reported in January, May, August, and December, and generated 30 reports. Table 1 lists the total numbers of netizens’ comments and reply comments, and the starting and ending time of each round of commenting and summarizes the content of the reports by month.
aiSummaries: Research Question The article examines three primary questions: (1) What is the diversity of Chinese netizens’ opinions regarding capital punishment in the Lin Senhao poisoning case? (2) What rationales do netizens provide in support of Lin’s death sentence? (3) What is the nature of interactions among netizens in online discussions? (pp. 674). The study aims to uncover the breadth, depth, and nuances of public opinion beyond traditional survey methods (pp. 667–668). Methods The authors employ qualitative thematic analysis to code and categorize online comments (p. 674). Data were collected in spring 2016 from Sina.com.cn, focusing on all 2015 death penalty case reports, with detailed analysis centered on the Lin Senhao case (pp. 670–671). Comments were grouped into thematic categories, and interactions (replies) were separately analyzed to assess deliberative dynamics (pp. 672, 682). Data The authors collected new data from online comment forums rather than using existing datasets (p. 670). The broader dataset included 64 death penalty cases (121 defendants), but this study focuses on Lin’s case, which generated 17,611 comments and 5,683 replies (pp. 671–673). Comments were geographically diverse, covering all provincial-level units in China (p. 671). Primary Legal Sources (PRC cases, statutes, legislation, regulations, judicial interpretations, international agreements, etc.) The primary legal source is the Lin Senhao case, adjudicated by the Shanghai Second Intermediate People’s Court, affirmed by the Shanghai High Court, and reviewed and approved by the Supreme People’s Court (pp. 669–670). The article references judicial review procedures but does not conduct doctrinal analysis of specific statutes. No specific statutory provisions are explicitly analyzed. Findings While most netizens supported Lin’s death sentence, opinions were diverse and often conflicting (pp. 686–687). Dominant rationales included retribution (sharen changming), deterrence, justice/legal necessity, and social impact (pp. 676–681). A minority questioned deterrence, retribution, and judicial populism, challenging claims of “overwhelming public support” (pp. 686–687). Interactions frequently involved counterarguments and personal attacks, with limited evidence of opinion change through deliberation (pp. 682–686). Limitations and Future Research The study acknowledges selection bias due to reliance on internet users and lack of demographic data (p. 686). Government censorship and unknown netizen identities present additional concerns (p. 686). The analysis focuses on a single murder case, limiting generalizability to other capital offenses (p. 686). Future research should examine additional case types and explore whether online deliberation can meaningfully influence attitudes toward capital punishment (pp. 686–687).

The Lin Senhao case generated 17,611 comments, including 5,683 reply comments. • Time Period Covered Cases reported between January 1 and December 31, 2015. Comments were coded using thematic analysis. Comments reflecting multiple themes were counted separately for each theme. • Stated Limitations of the Dataset Selection bias; lack of individual and personal background information on netizens; uncertainty regarding netizen identity; possible governmental censorship; and analysis limited to one murder case.

title: A Never-Ending U.S. - China Solar Trade War? The Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act And International Trade Law
citation: Mandy Meng Fang, A Never-Ending U.S.-China Solar Trade War? The Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act and International Trade Law, 33, Minnesota Journal of International Law, 189 (2024).
historicalResearch: Y: It compares UFLPA with previous U.S. legislations concerning restricting China's rare material.
comparativeResearch: Y: It compares UFLPA with eariler legislations on their reach and magnitude of policy implications.
interdisciplinaryResearch: Y: It looks at the U.S.-China conflict's implication on trade regulations around the world (WTO) and also on envrionmental sustainability
aiSummaries: Research Question The article asks whether the Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act (UFLPA)—as applied to solar photovoltaic (PV) products connected to Xinjiang—comports with U.S. obligations under the World Trade Organization (WTO) regime, particularly under the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT). (pp. 190–192) It further examines how UFLPA enforcement may affect U.S.–China solar trade and the broader solar supply chain during the low-carbon transition. (pp. 190–192) Methods The author develops arguments from “law and policy perspectives,” expressly noting that legal analysis alone is insufficient for the research questions. (p. 192) The article proceeds in four parts: background on the solar trade conflict and UFLPA; application of GATT disciplines (notably Articles XI and I) to assess consistency; evaluation of potential defenses under GATT Article XX; and recommendations for the United States and China. (pp. 192–193) Data Not explicitly specified in the article as a dataset-based empirical study. The analysis relies on legal texts, enforcement guidance, and reported trade impacts (e.g., shipment detentions and installation changes) as discussed in the article. (pp. 190–191, 197–201, 219–221) Primary Legal Sources (PRC cases, statutes, legislation, regulations, judicial interpretations, international agreements, etc.) Key sources include the UFLPA (signed Dec. 23, 2021; effective June 2022), CBP operational guidance and the Forced Labor Enforcement Task Force (FLETF) strategy, and the Section 307 framework under the Tariff Act of 1930 and subsequent U.S. trade enforcement changes. (pp. 196–200) The WTO/GATT provisions analyzed include Articles XI, I (MFN), and XX (including chapeau requirements). (pp. 203–219) Additional legal references include the Trafficking Victims Protection Act and ILO Convention No. 105, as well as climate-policy references (e.g., Paris Agreement commitments and the Inflation Reduction Act) in the policy discussion. (pp. 210–214, 220–224) Findings The article argues the UFLPA functions as an import restriction that contravenes GATT Article XI and discriminates under GATT Article I because it targets goods connected to Xinjiang. (pp. 203–207) It contends that UFLPA is unlikely to be justified under Article XX(a) or XX(d) due to “necessity” concerns, and in any event risks failing the Article XX chapeau because it is region-specific and therefore arbitrarily or unjustifiably discriminatory. (pp. 207–219) The article also links stringent UFLPA enforcement to disruption in U.S. solar supply and potential setbacks to U.S. deployment and decarbonization trajectories. (pp. 219–222) Limitations and Future Research Not explicitly specified in the article as a limitations/future research section. The author notes uncertainty about how WTO adjudicators may apply deference under evolving jurisprudence, implying that legal outcomes are contingent and context-dependent. (pp. 214–216) The article’s recommendations suggest future work could further examine alternative regulatory designs and enforcement mechanisms that better balance labor-rights objectives with trade disciplines. (pp. 223–225)

title: Authoritarian Legal (Ir)Rationality: The Saga Of 'Picking Quarrels' In China
citation: Jiajun Luo, Authoritarian Legal (Ir)rationality: The Saga of "Picking Quarrels' in China, 25, Asian-Pacific Law and Policy Journal, 103 (2024)
abstract: This paper seeks to enhance our understanding of authoritarian legal systems and broaden our comprehension of the generic concept of legality. It explores ongoing discussions about the authoritarian legality of the People’s Republic of China. While China seems to be "turning to law" in the present context, it is crucial to examine the nature and quality of the authoritarian legality. This paper introduces the concept of "legal rationality," signifying a state where the discretion of dominant officials is constrained by legal rules that are publicly accessible, clear, and consistent. In this paper, I argue that legal rationality is a fundamental element for any functional legal system, even within an authoritarian framework. The paper examines legal rationality in post-Mao Zedong China, with a specific focus on its decline under the administration of Xi Jinping. In doing so, the paper challenges the conventional assumption of “dual state”, which posits that the existence and expansion of prerogative power in an authoritarian system are confined solely to its politicized sphere, marked by political prosecutions. Instead, this article argues that the decline of legal rationality in an authoritarian legal system leads to the arbitrary discretion of dominant officials infiltrating both political and non-political domains. It illustrates this point through the example of "picking quarrels and provoking trouble," a notorious catch-all crime exploited by Chinese authorities in both political prosecutions and routine criminal cases. This contributes to our understanding of authoritarian legality and demonstrates how the erosion of legal rationality has adversely impacted both the politicized realm and routine legal order in Xi’s China.
conclusion: In 2014, a renowned Chinese law expert, Stanley Lubman, commented that the larger significance of Picking Quarrels prosecutions is “the shadow that they cast over public expression of views deemed to be offensive, especially because they coincide with campaigns to limit public debate by lawyers and journalists.”349 Nine years later, that shadow has only grown larger. Today, not only are lawyers, journalists, or other individuals who are deemed as a threat to authoritarian rule by the Party, but ordinary citizens uninvolved in politics also find themselves living under the shadow of arbitrary measures from Chinese authorities, all in the name of Picking Quarrels. Picking Quarrels, once considered a tool of reform, has evolved under Xi Jinping’s administration into an arbitrary punitive regime, allowing for the arrest and prosecution of almost anyone deemed objectionable by the authorities. There are indeed voices inside China advocating for reforming Picking Quarrels, yet they seem to have limited impact to halt the downward spiral. For example, in May 2023, Zhu Zhengfu, a Chinese lawyer and delegate to the National People’s Congress, China’s top legislature, advocated for the abolition of the offense due to its extensive abuse. Despite these efforts, however, such voices have been disregarded thus far, leading to no tangible reforms. In August 2023, an internal research group reported to the SPC that Picking Quarrels has been abused by local government to crackdown petitioners. 351 Nevertheless, similar to Zhu's proposal, there has been no constructive response from the legislature, SPC leadership, or any high-ranking officials indicating that meaningful reform of this catch-all crime is on the horizon. The saga of Picking Quarrels exemplifies that a decline in legal rationality is not limited to the political sphere; it has also affected China’s routine criminal justice and everyday governance system352. In Xi’s China, the line between the political and non-political realms is becoming increasingly blurred. On one hand, we are witnessing a surge in arbitrariness in political prosecutions under Xi. With the introduction of the overarching framework of “Comprehensive National Security,” political suppression is expanding beyond targeting large protests, collective petitions, and other “mass incidents” that might pose threats to political and social stability. It now extends to severely curb almost all forms of public participation and free speech. On the other hand, the arbitrariness stemming from the prerogative powers of the state is steadily corroding the routine governance system that is supposed to be based on established legal principles. In summary, this article argues that the decline of legal rationality in authoritarian legal system not only increases arbitrariness within the political domain but may also permeate routine legal order. In other words, the preoperative nature of authoritarian law, is not strictly confined to the political sphere. Thus, this article encourages a reevaluation of our understanding of authoritarian legal systems, advocating for a nuanced reconsideration of the simplistic binary view often applied to authoritarian law. Ultimately, the conclusion drawn is that authoritarian legality is more accurately defined by the (ir)rationality of the law rather than solely its political dimensions.
researchQuestions: "Despite ongoing scholarly debates, the importance of law in governing authoritarian systems is widely acknowledged. With this in mind, it becomes crucial to grasp the essence and characteristics of authoritarian law. This article introduces the concept of legal rationality to the discussion of authoritarian legality."
answers: "It proposes that, whether a system is democratic or authoritarian, legal rationality denotes the intrinsic value of law that is publicly accessible, transparent, and consistent, serving to restrict the arbitrary discretion of individuals in positions of authority. Thus, if there is a decline in legal rationality within an authoritarian system, it may not only result in more political prosecutions but could also lead to increased arbitrariness in non-political domains as well."

aiSummaries: Research Question The article asks how the offense of “Picking Quarrels and Provoking Trouble” (Article 293 of the 1997 PRC Criminal Code) illustrates the nature and evolution of “authoritarian legality” in contemporary China (pp. 104–109). Specifically, it examines whether the boundary between political and non-political spheres meaningfully constrains arbitrary power in China’s legal system (pp. 107–108). The central inquiry is whether the decline of legal rationality under Xi Jinping explains both intensified political repression and growing arbitrariness in routine criminal justice (pp. 124–125). Methods The article adopts a doctrinal and theoretical legal analysis. It introduces the concept of “legal rationality” and situates it within debates on authoritarian legality and the “dual state” thesis (pp. 110–118). The author conducts a historical and doctrinal examination of hooliganism and its successor offense, Picking Quarrels, across Mao, reform-era, and Xi periods (pp. 126–137). Case illustrations from court verdicts and judicial interpretations are used to demonstrate patterns of application (pp. 138–144). Data The study does not rely on a quantitative dataset. It analyzes statutory texts, judicial interpretations, court verdicts, and reported cases. Primary materials include Article 293 of the 1997 Criminal Code, the 1979 Criminal Code, Judicial Interpretation No. 18 (2013), and Judicial Interpretation No. 21 (2013) (pp. 134–144). The author relies on publicly available verdicts and documented cases; no new empirical dataset is collected. Primary Legal Sources (PRC cases, statutes, legislation, regulations, judicial interpretations, international agreements, etc.) Key sources include the 1979 PRC Criminal Code (Art. 160, hooliganism), the 1997 PRC Criminal Code (Art. 293, Picking Quarrels), and subsequent SPC/SPP judicial interpretations (pp. 129–135, 143–144). The article also references political cases (e.g., Tiananmen-related prosecutions, feminist activists, petitioners, online speech cases) and discusses the broader national security framework under Xi (pp. 138–143). Findings The article argues that post-Mao reforms partially rationalized criminal law by clarifying elements, reducing penalties, and limiting analogy (pp. 133–136). Under Xi, however, Picking Quarrels has been repoliticized and expanded—particularly into online speech—through vague interpretations, reflecting declining legal rationality (pp. 138–144). The offense now functions as a political catchall while also expanding arbitrary discretion in non-political cases, eroding predictability and constraint in the legal system (pp. 124–125). Limitations and Future Research Not explicitly specified in the article. The analysis is primarily doctrinal and theoretical, suggesting future research could further empirically examine patterns of application and the broader implications of declining legal rationality.

It asks how each bloc’s legal framework—particularly concerning cross-border data transfers—advances strategic objectives such as national security, economic competitiveness, and fundamental rights protection (pp. 5–8). The study seeks to provide a holistic assessment of these regulatory regimes and their interaction (pp. 1–2). Methods The authors employ comparative legal analysis across three major jurisdictions: the United States, the European Union, and China (pp. 1–2). The study examines statutory frameworks, judicial decisions (e.g., Schrems II), regulatory measures, and policy developments in light of geopolitical considerations (pp. 24–30). title: Coded Social Control: China’S Normalization Of Biometric Surveillance In The Post Covid-19 Era
citation: Michelle Miao, Coded Social Control: China's Normalization of Biometric Surveillance in the Post Covid-19 Era, 19 Wash. J.L. Tech. & Arts 53 (2024)
journalTitle: Washington Journal of Law, Technology & Arts
abstract: This article investigates the longevity of health QR codes, a digital instrument of pandemic surveillance, in post-COVID China. From 2020 to 2022, China widely used this tri-color tool to combat the COVID-19 pandemic. A commonly held assumption is that health QR codes have become obsolete in post-pandemic China. This study challenges such an assumption. It reveals their persistence and integration-through mobile apps and online platforms-beyond the COVID-19 public health emergency. A prolonged, expanded and normalized use of tools which were originally intended for contact tracing and pandemic surveillance raises critical legal and ethical concerns. Moreover, their functional transformation from epidemiological risk assessment tools to instruments of behavior modification and social governance heralds the emergence of a Data Leviathan. This transformation is underpinned by a duality of underlying political and commercial forces. These include 1) a structural enabler: a powerful alliance between political authorities and tech giants and 2) an ideological legitimizer: a commitment to collective security over individual autonomy. In contrast to the rights-centric approach embraced by Western democracies to regulate AI-driven biometric surveillance, China adopts a state-industry dominance model of governance.
conclusion: The coronavirus pandemic has no doubt accelerated the arrival of this machine- dominated era where human governance succumbs to algorithmic rules. “I had believed that the days when we, as humans, would be governed by machines and algorithms was supposed to be at least half a century away. However, the sudden outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic has accelerated its arrival… with a health red code issued by big data and machines,” wrote by a blogger on China’s popular knowledge-exchange forum Zhihu.216 In an act of self-irony, the blogger described himself as “a red code holder under the tyranny of big data and machines” and expressed his helplessness and frustration, like many others, who had no option but to “wait for machines to grant me freedom.”217 We stand at a historical juncture when surveillance has never before exerted such a “lightweight” yet profoundly and pervasive impact over every aspect of human existence, spanning from the physical to the mental realms, as it does today. China’s health QR codes is a pioneering tool which signals this ambitious fusion between the dictates of political authority and the power of algorithms. Contrary to the assumption that biometric surveillance enabled by big data and AI has vanished from the post-pandemic life is ample evidence that the coded control of health QR codes continues to offer a subtle art of sanction. This article critically examines China’s health QR code system against the fragmented and deficient legal-regulatory framework governing its operation and the ethical tenets that define the moral responsibilities of data processors. While the pandemic's exigencies spurred rapid adoption of these nascent systems without rigorous safeguards, their persistence after the pandemic has been enabled by a concerted collaboration between state and tech powers as well and legitimized by an ideological commitment to safeguard collective security. As such, China’s pandemic responses prioritize public health over individual liberties and privacy, and political as well commercial interests over personal rights, thereby resulting in a state-industry dominance model of pandemic governance, in contrast to the rights-oriented approach adopted by Western democracies. This explains why China’s legal system plays such a limited role in reshaping the dynamics and balance between power, technology, and individual rights. The COVID-19 pandemic underscores advanced technology's capacity to either empower or subordinate humanity - depending on whether its arc bends toward justice. The interplay between technology and ethical accountability prompts a fascinating inquiry: Is it the innovation of technology that diminishes our sense of responsibility, or is it an inherent desire to evade moral duty that spurs the adoption of emerging technologies? This present study suggests that the real problem, perhaps, is not whether surveillance technology diminishes our autonomy, privacy and rights. Rather, it is the desire, political and commercial, to be relieved of moral responsibility that drives the adoption and perpetuation of new technology.

authorsDataDescription: The personal and biometric information—on which big data analytic techniques were applied to compute health QR codes—were gleaned from diverse public and private repositories. Initially, users were required to self-report personal information, biometrics, travel history, medical records, and exposure risks via WeChat and Alipay platforms. Subsequently, government and corporation-controlled metadata—including mobile network traffic data, migration data, and GPS travel history—were conscripted. The sources for this data ranged from China’s major telecommunication operators to local traffic authorities. In April 2020, a published national technical standard set out a comprehensive taxonomy of personal data collected and fed into the health QR code algorithms.
aiSummaries: Research Question The article investigates whether China’s health QR code system—originally developed as a pandemic surveillance tool—has been normalized and integrated into broader systems of biometric surveillance and social governance in the post-COVID-19 era (pp. 53–55). It challenges the assumption that health QR codes became obsolete after the pandemic and instead asks how they persist as instruments of behavioral regulation and social control (pp. 53–56). The study further examines the legal, ethical, and political implications of this transformation (pp. 65–66). Methods The study adopts doctrinal legal analysis and socio-legal inquiry. It reviews national statutes, administrative regulations, and local rules governing biometric data and public health emergency responses (pp. 65–69). It also conducts normative ethical analysis, identifying tensions between data protection principles and executive discretion (pp. 72–86). The article situates legal developments within broader theoretical frameworks concerning surveillance, governance, and authoritarian ideology (pp. 86–90). Data The article does not rely on an empirical dataset. It analyzes legislative texts (e.g., Civil Code, Personal Information Protection Law, Cybersecurity Law, infectious disease laws), administrative rules concerning health QR codes, government policy documents, and reported cases of QR code usage (pp. 66–70). The author uses existing legal materials and documented policy practices rather than collecting new empirical data. Primary Legal Sources (PRC cases, statutes, legislation, regulations, judicial interpretations, international agreements, etc.) Key sources include the Personal Information Protection Law (2021), Civil Code (2020), Cybersecurity Law (2017), Criminal Law, Law on Prevention and Treatment of Infectious Diseases, Emergency Response Law, and related administrative rules issued under the State Council’s Joint Prevention and Control Mechanism (pp. 66–69). The article also references local regulations and technical standards governing health QR codes (pp. 58–63). Constitutional provisions concerning human dignity, liberty, and human rights are discussed as background norms (p. 67). Findings The study finds that health QR codes have shifted from temporary epidemiological tools to normalized instruments of digital social governance (pp. 61–63). This transformation reflects both structural enablers—state–tech alliances—and ideological prioritization of collective security over individual autonomy (pp. 86–89). Legal safeguards exist in form but are undermined by broad emergency exceptions, administrative discretion, and weak enforcement (pp. 66–70). The result is the emergence of a “Data Leviathan” characterized by pervasive biometric surveillance (pp. 53–55). Limitations and Future Research Not explicitly specified in the article. The study is primarily doctrinal and theoretical; future empirical research could further examine the long-term societal impacts and implementation practices of biometric governance in post-pandemic China.

aiSummaries: **Research Question** The study examines two interrelated questions: (1) the extent and characteristics of confessions and torture, and (2) the goals of extracting confessions through torture in China’s crime control model (p. S33) . It situates these questions within tensions between truth-finding and finality under an inquisitorial system (pp. S33–S34) . **Methods** The authors conduct an exploratory analysis of 103 wrongfully convicted death penalty cases from 1983 to 2012 (p. S33) . Cases were identified through the “Wrongful Conviction” website and supplemented by court documents, news reports, and blogs (p. S39) . Variables relating to offenders, offenses, confessions, and torture were coded; torture was coded based on defense claims and categorized into physical and psychological forms (p. S40) . Cases were also classified into “truth-finding” and “finality” groups depending on whether evidence fabrication or manipulation occurred (p. S46) . **Data** The study relies on existing case data compiled by a private law firm’s “Wrongful Conviction” website (p. S39) . The final sample includes 103 exonerated capital cases involving violent crimes (p. S39) . Supplementary information was drawn from publicly available judicial documents and media sources (p. S39) . **Primary Legal Sources (PRC cases, statutes, legislation, regulations, judicial interpretations, international agreements, etc.)** The article analyzes the 1979 Criminal Law and Criminal Procedure Law (CPL), which prohibited torture and outlined procedural safeguards (p. S35) . Amendments to the CPL in 1996 and 2012 expanded procedural protections, including recording interrogations and exclusionary rules (pp. S36, S38) . It also references Supreme People’s Procuratorate provisions regarding torture-related misconduct (p. S37) . **Findings** Confessions were obtained in 90% of cases, and 94% involved torture (p. S41) . Physical torture predominated, though psychological methods increased after 1996 (p. S43) . Approximately three-fourths of cases involved police misconduct such as evidence fabrication, suggesting that finality often superseded truth-finding (pp. S46–S47) . Accountability was limited, with only about 20% of officers punished (p. S46) . **Limitations and Future Research** The dataset is unofficial and not comprehensive (p. S39) . Missing data and limited access to official records constrained verification of torture claims (p. S40) . The authors call for stronger exclusionary rules, improved recording practices, enhanced accountability, and systemic error-correction mechanisms to reduce coerced confessions (pp. S48–S50) .

title: Continuation Of Systemic Failures: From Cui Ning To Contemporary Wrongful Conviction Cases In China
citation: Ming Hu & Bin Liang, Continuation of Systemic Failures: From Cui Ning to Contemporary Wrongful Conviction Cases in China, 17 Asian J. Criminology (Supp. 1) 75 (2022)
abstract: The wrongful conviction of Cui Ning is a famous short novel first recorded in the Song dynasty. It tells of a story about the wrongful conviction and execution of two innocent people, Cui Ning and madam Chen. In this study, we pay particular attention to systemic failures that led to the miscarriage of justice in this case, including presumption of guilt, use of torture, improper influence of public opinion, lack of effective defense rights and appeal procedures, and lack of checks and balances within the criminal justice system. We further examine continuation of these systemic failures in four contemporary wrongful conviction cases including Teng Xingshan (1987), She Xianglin (1994), Nie Shubin (1995), and Huugjilt (1996). The continuation of these systemic failures provides important lessons to China’s contemporary criminal justice system.

researchQuestions: In this study, we pay particular attention to systemic failures that led to the miscarriage of justice in this case, including presumption of guilt, use of torture, improper influence of public opinion, lack of effective defense rights and appeal procedures, and lack of checks and balances within the criminal justice system. We further examine continuation of these systemic failures in four contemporary wrongful conviction cases including Teng Xingshan (1987), She Xianglin (1994), Nie Shubin (1995), and Huugjilt (1996). The continuation of these systemic failures provides important lessons to China’s contemporary criminal justice system.
answers: We argue that there is continuity of systemic failures despite passage of time. Specifically, we identify five systemic failures in a famous story, the wrongful execution of Cui Ning (cuozhan Cui Ning), from the Song dynasty (960–1279), and contrast them with four contemporary cases to examine the continuation of such failures. We argue that systemic failures witnessed in the Cui Ning case, albeit with modernized forms, continued to plague the practice of contemporary Chinese criminal justice.

aiSummaries: **Research Question** The article examines whether systemic failures that led to the wrongful conviction and execution of Cui Ning in the Song dynasty continue to manifest in contemporary Chinese wrongful conviction cases (pp. S75–S77). Specifically, it identifies five systemic failures in the Cui Ning case and analyzes their persistence in four modern cases: Teng Xingshan, She Xianglin, Nie Shubin, and Huugjilt (pp. S76, S79–S80). The study seeks to draw lessons for reforming China’s criminal justice system (pp. S75–S76). **Methods** The authors employ a qualitative comparative case study method (pp. S76, S79–S80). They analyze a historical fictional case (Cui Ning) alongside four officially acknowledged contemporary wrongful conviction cases (pp. S79–S80). The study triangulates judicial judgments, academic literature, newspaper reports, and materials from a Chinese innocence project website to assess patterns of systemic failure (p. S80). **Data** The authors rely on existing data rather than collecting new empirical data (p. S80). Sources include partial judicial judgments (except for Nie’s full judgment), 34 law journals, 48 social science journals, 69 university journals, six major newspapers, and materials from the innocence project website (p. S80). The Cui Ning case is drawn from historical literary collections and prior scholarly analyses (pp. S76–S77). **Primary Legal Sources (PRC cases, statutes, legislation, regulations, judicial interpretations, international agreements, etc.)** Primary sources include judicial judgments in the four contemporary cases (p. S80). The Criminal Procedure Law (CPL), particularly Article 53 on evidentiary standards and provisions prohibiting torture (Articles 32/43/50/52 in successive versions), is discussed (pp. S82–S83). The 2010 Evidence Rules and the 2012 and 2018 CPL amendments are referenced (pp. S89–S90). International instruments include the Convention against Torture (ratified) and the ICCPR (not ratified) (p. S91). **Findings** Five systemic failures—presumption of guilt, torture, public pressure, weak defense and appeals, and lack of checks and balances—were present in the Cui Ning case and persist in modern cases (pp. S77–S79, S80–S88). Contemporary manifestations include coerced confessions, assembly-line coordination among police, procuratorate, and courts, political interventions, and weakened death penalty review during “strike-hard” campaigns (pp. S82–S88). Reforms have been introduced but remain limited and compromised by crime-control priorities (pp. S89–S91). **Limitations and Future Research** The authors acknowledge that the four cases are illustrative rather than exhaustive and represent only the “tip of an iceberg” (p. S89). They note the difficulty of detecting wrongful convictions absent dramatic reversals (pp. S86, S89). Future research should further examine political and cultural influences, comparative historical developments, and structural reforms to address enduring systemic flaws (pp. S90–S91).

title: Death Sentence Review By The Supreme People'S Court In China: Decision Patterns And Variations
authors: Moulin Xiong, Siyu Liu, Bin Liang
citation: Moulin Xiong, Siyu Liu & Bin Liang, Death Sentence Review by the Supreme People’s Court in China: Decision Patterns and Variations, 22 China Rev. 137 (2022)
journalTitle: Jstor (Published By: The Chinese University of Hong Kong Press)
abstract: China has been making efforts to reduce the number of executions via a recentralized review process under judicial guidelines. This study analyzes 650 Death Sentence Review Transcripts collected from China Judgments Online, aiming to decipher patterns and variations of death review decisions via descriptive facts and circumstances. We find that marital or domestic violence, offender's guilty conscience or forgiveness by victim's family, and insufficient heinousness underline the disapproval patterns. In contrast, large number of victims or victim deaths, use of weapon, brutal crime nature, being a ringleader in violent crimes, and significant drug weight in drug crimes describe the approval patterns with a general lack of recognition for mitigating circumstances. However, significant variations existed among approved cases, involving defendants who caused a single death for domestic or marital dispute, had no criminal record, committed a crime of passion, or carried low-purity drugs or acted as drug mules. These variations raise the question of whether the Supreme People's Court maintains a consistent standard following its own guidelines. In China, every capital case in which a defendant faces execution is reviewed by the Supreme People’s Court (hereinafter SPC), after the authority to review and approve was recentralized in 2007.1 For defen- dants who are sentenced to the death penalty with immediate execution, the Criminal Procedure Law (CPL) stipulates a special procedure called death sentence review (DSR). Although the SPC has been the final arbiter of whether a defendant is to be executed since 2007, a difficult question concerns how to evaluate the effect of the reformed DSR. One way is to simply examine SPC’s affirmation (or disapproval) rate over time. The numbers of death sentences reportedly decreased signifi- cantly in the first recentralized year,2 nevertheless the effect of DSR might be rather limited in reducing executions over a long period of time. For instance, the remand rate for retrial or resentencing was about 29 percent in the early 2000s,3 15 percent (or higher) in 2007,4 but decreased to 2–10 percent in the 2010s.5 If these numbers closely reflected the reality, the declining reversal rate could cast some doubt on the performance of the recentralized DSR. Alternatively, we could study SPC’s review standards. Often, the public questioned review decisions based on the reporting of high-profile cases. For instance, in the midst of the execution of Jia Jinglong (賈敬龍), some scholars and lawyers sent a petition letter to SPC Chief Justice Zhou Qiang (周強) and challenged the legality of the demolition of Jia’s house and petitioned to spare his life.6 In contrast, under the pressure of national and international anti-death penalty movements, the SPC remanded the death sentence of Li Yan (李彥), a victim of domestic violence who killed and dismembered her husband.7 Indeed, DSR is a powerful but contentious process. Compared to general criminal proceedings, two unique DSR features offer some insights to SPC’s review procedure. The first feature is the atypical inter- rogative process. Unlike trials of first and second instances, DSR is not an open trial but a review of written documents.8 The second feature is the lack of legal representation for defendants.9 Past studies found a rather low representation rate—less than 13 percent.10 These two features leave ample room to question the DSR’s functional performance. In this study, we examine 650 Criminal Transcripts of DSR (TDSR; 死刑復核刑事裁定書 sixing fuhe xingshi caiding shu) involving 701 capital defendants to dissect SPC’s review decisions, and explore its decision rationales and potential issues. Given the major limitations of TDSR publications (e.g., being selective due to lack of transparency on publication standards),11 we do not intend to decipher the exact number of executions every year, nor to define the punitive norms of death sentences in China.12 Rather, based on published TDSRs, we aim to capi- talize on the rich information from cases in our sample to shed light on SPC’s review decisions by identifying patterns from real cases against SPC’s published guidelines and digests, and to draw some policy implica- tions regarding this unique practice.
conclusion: "To summarize, this study shows considerable variations among SPC's DSRs, and there is still a long way to go to fulfill the policy of ""killing fewer"" instructed by SPC's official slogan. Note that DSR must be advised by SPC Adjudication Committee in some cases, which we cannot observe from the published TDSRs.49 By any means, the SPC should keep improving the consistency of its decisions and making the use of death sentences more predictable. We caution the reader regarding the lack of generalizability of our data. TDPRs utilized in this study were not complete collections of all death sentenced cases in the related years or from a certain jurisdiction. We also acknowledge the methodological limitations of descriptive analysis due to limitation of data in current reality. More inferential statistical analysis (e.g., multivariate analysis) would be helpful in the [End Page 159] future once more data becomes available. Fully acknowledging these caveats, we argue that the wide range of cases in our sample allows a limited but meaningful analysis of the DSR decision patterns and the extent of their variations, which is not achieved in previous studies, thus our unique contribution to the readership of death sentence in China. We believe that the results of our study shed light on our understanding the DSR process, particularly through analyzing cases that are comparable based on legal factors such as the type of offense, the level of harm, and the background of the offender. The identified deviations from SPC guidelines could be a starting point for review of actual DSR practices and potential policy changes to further calibrate the death sentencing process in China. Given the finding that even elite SPC judges struggled to maintain uniform and consistent standards in DSR, one critical step would be to revise the relevant content of the CL to better guide judicial decisions from meting out death sentences. We hope this study helps inspire further intellectual inquiries and contribute to meaningful policy improvements regarding China's death penalty practice."

aiSummaries: Research Question The article examines how the Supreme People’s Court (SPC) in China conducts Death Sentence Review (DSR) after the 2007 recentralization of review authority (pp. 137–139). It asks whether the SPC maintains consistent standards in approving or disapproving immediate death sentences, particularly in light of its own judicial guidelines (pp. 140–143). The study seeks to identify decision patterns and variations in violent and drug-related capital cases (pp. 142–143). Methods The study employs a systematic content-based descriptive analysis of published Death Sentence Review Transcripts (TDSRs) (pp. 143–145). The authors manually coded case information using a structured codebook, with multiple rounds of verification to ensure consistency (p. 144). Due to data limitations and lack of representativeness, the analysis relies on descriptive rather than inferential statistical methods (p. 145). Data The authors collected 650 TDSRs (involving 701 capital defendants) retrieved from China Judgments Online between June 2016 and July 2018 (pp. 143–144). The cases span 2013–2016 and cover 30 provinces in mainland China (p. 144). The study uses existing publicly available judicial transcripts rather than collecting new primary data; drug quantities were standardized using official transformation formulas to convert substances into heroin equivalents (p. 145). Primary Legal Sources (PRC cases, statutes, legislation, regulations, judicial interpretations, international agreements, etc.) Key sources include the Criminal Law (Arts. 232, 236, 239, 347, 48), the Criminal Procedure Law, and SPC judicial guidelines and conference digests on violent and drug crimes (pp. 139–141). The study also references SPC interpretations and internal guidance documents governing aggravating and mitigating circumstances (pp. 139–141). Findings The authors identify patterns in disapproved cases, including marital or domestic disputes, offender remorse, victim forgiveness, and insufficient heinousness (pp. 147–149). Approval patterns emphasize multiple victim deaths, brutal methods, use of weapons, leadership roles in gangs, and large drug quantities (pp. 150–152). However, significant variations exist, including approved cases involving single-victim murders, first-time offenders, crimes of passion, or relatively lower drug weights (pp. 153–158). These inconsistencies raise concerns about sentencing predictability and adherence to SPC guidelines (pp. 158–159). Limitations and Future Research The study acknowledges that TDSRs represent only a selectively published subset of all reviewed cases, limiting generalizability (p. 159). The absence of complete national execution data and limited variation in outcomes constrain inferential statistical analysis (pp. 145, 159). Future research could apply multivariate methods if more comprehensive data become available (p. 159).

title: Does Bankruptcy Law Prevent Internalization Of Environmental Costs: Evidence From China
citation: Xingwei Li Xingwei Li, Does Bankruptcy Law Prevent Internalization of Environmental Costs: Evidence from China, 23 J. Int’l Bus. & L. 159 (2024)
journalTitle: Journal of International Business and Law
abstract: (No abstract just intro) In 2019, the Intermediate People’s Court of Qiqihar City, Heilongjiang Province, China declared Heihua Co., Ltd (“Heihua”) bankrupt.1 Despite the fact that Heihua had made great contributions to economic development within the Heilongjiang province, it was also at fault for discharging vast amounts of smoke, dust, and heavy metals into the surrounding land and air which caused subsequent environmental damage, in terms of land and air pollution, as well as water contamination.2 Bankruptcy was attributable to these environmental issues, as well as the firm’s aging equipment, yet the aforementioned environmental issues were not resolved following the completion of the bankruptcy process; with the soil and groundwater at the former Heihua site containing a large quantity of heavy metals far in excess of national standards, the site was later identified as heavily contaminated.3 Cleanup and restoration of the contaminated site is in the public’s best interest.4 As the liquidation process of the bankrupt company has since concluded, it follows that any future rectification of consequential environmental damage stemming from the Heihua site must be covered by public funds.5 This highlights a common issue: the polluting company will be rendered bankrupt with several of its environmental issues still outstanding and pass any such liability on to the public—in other words, the cost of environmental liability will be borne by public funds.6
conclusion: The polluter pays principle is explicitly detailed within Chinese environmental legislation, but this principle has been challenged in the context of bankruptcy law. 442 In practice, environmental costs have been transferred to society in bankruptcy proceedings, with many contaminated sites not having been cleaned up post-liquidation. 443 Some companies use bankruptcy regimes to escape their environmental responsibilities. 444 The enactment of EBL 2006 was a success in that it solved the problem of bankruptcy in China. 445 However, EBL 2006 has now become a haven for polluters insofar as environmental resources have become a somewhat free cost for companies under the bankruptcy framework in China. 446 From the perspective of the polluter pays principle, EBL 2006 has thus revealed a number of problems, such as a lack of environmental representation in bankruptcy proceedings, as well as environmental liabilities not being given priority in the winding up process. EBL 2006 should consider several changes to realize the internalization of environmental costs, such as giving priority to environmental liability in winding up and increasing the presence of environmental representatives in bankruptcy proceedings, which are recommendations on bankruptcy proceedings. 447 Meanwhile, the corporate entity post-liquidation is also an important way to internalize environmental costs. Therefore, the corporate veil should be lifted when there is sufficient evidence that a company’s shareholders have deliberately transferred property to avoid environmental liability, or that corporate accounts have been mixed with private accounts.

authorsDataDescription: The case study section of this Article describes how Chinese law treats environmental liability in bankruptcy proceedings and considers three core findings as were discussed in the thesis, Environmental Liabilities and Insolvent Polluters in China Learning Lessons from the UK and US.
aiSummaries: **Research Question** The article examines whether China’s Enterprise Bankruptcy Law 2006 (EBL 2006) prevents the internalization of environmental costs by allowing environmental liabilities of bankrupt polluters to be externalized to the public (pp. 159–160) . It specifically asks how Chinese bankruptcy law treats environmental liabilities in insolvency proceedings and whether reform is needed to ensure polluters bear cleanup costs (p. 161) . **Methods** The article employs doctrinal legal analysis of the EBL 2006 and related environmental legislation (pp. 161–165) . It supplements this analysis with qualitative case studies of four Chinese enterprises—Shenyang Smelter, Heilongjiang Heihua Co., Yunnan Yongren Tuanshan Copper Mine, and Yunnan Jinggu Mining (pp. 165–176) . The cases are used to illustrate how environmental liabilities are treated in practice during liquidation and reorganization. **Data** The study relies on existing legal texts, corporate reports, government announcements, court decisions, and media sources. It analyzes statutory materials such as the EBL 2006 and Environmental Protection Law 2014 (pp. 159–160) , as well as publicly available financial reports and case documents concerning the selected enterprises (pp. 168–170) . No new empirical dataset was collected; the analysis is based on documentary and case evidence. **Primary Legal Sources (PRC cases, statutes, legislation, regulations, judicial interpretations, international agreements, etc.)** Key legal sources include the Enterprise Bankruptcy Law 2006 (pp. 161–163) , the Civil Code 2020 (Tort chapter), and the Environmental Protection Law 2014, which enshrine the “polluter pays” principle (p. 160) . The article also references Article 43 and 113 of the EBL concerning priority of claims (p. 165) and judicial provisions on environmental public interest litigation (p. 176) . **Findings** Environmental liabilities are not listed as priority claims under the EBL 2006 and are treated as general unsecured claims (p. 165) . In liquidation, environmental costs are often unpaid and subsequently borne by public funds, as shown in the Shenyang Smelter and Tuanshan cases (pp. 168, 175) . The article concludes that the EBL framework enables the externalization of environmental costs, undermining the polluter pays principle (pp. 159–160) . **Limitations and Future Research** The article does not present quantitative empirical data; its conclusions are based on selected case studies and doctrinal analysis. Not explicitly specified in the article regarding methodological limitations. The author proposes reform of the EBL to better internalize environmental costs in bankruptcy proceedings (p. 161) .

title: Elite Capture: How Decentralization And Informal Institutions Weaken Property Rights In China
citation: Daniel Mattingly, Elite Capture: How Decentralization and Informal Institutions Weaken Property Rights in China, 68(3) World Politics, 383-412 (2016).
abstract: Political decentralization is often argued to strengthen political accountability by bringing government closer to the people. Social and civic institutions at the local level, such as lineage associations, temples, churches, or social clubs, can make it easier for citizens to monitor officials and hold them accountable. This article argues that strong social institutions also empower local elites who may use their informal influence to control their group and capture rents. Drawing on evidence from case studies of Chinese villages, the article shows that lineage group leaders who become village officials use their combination of social and political authority to confiscate villagers’ land. Evidence from a survey experiment suggests that endorsement of a land confiscation plan by lineage elites elicits greater compliance with property seizures. A national survey indicates that when a lineage leader becomes a village cadre, it is associated with a 14 to 20 percent increase in the likelihood of a land expropriation. The findings demonstrate how informal institutions and local civil society can be tools of top-down political control.
conclusion: the lesson of this article is simple: local civil society groups often serve as tools of political control, and cannot replace formal institutions of accountability. as work by ostrom and tsai shows, nonstate groups can to a certain extent help members police themselves, especially in the context of repeated interaction.58 When these groups are truly autonomous, they can even help curb state predation. For example, similar to the findings presented here, timothy Frye finds that business organizations in russia can help protect private property.59 But the incorporation of the leaders of these groups into state institutions does not represent real political inclusion, rather, it is a form of co-optation. these findings show how seemingly democratic institutions can serve as institutions of co-optation even at the lowest level of politics. the literature to date has shown how regimes co-opt elites in national legislatures60 and how authoritarian institutions help the regime distribute rents and resolve conflict between political factions.61 Scholars of Chinese politics have also investigated how the regime has experimented with feedback mechanisms, transparency, and limited press freedoms to gather information about the behavior of local officials.62 Yet the logic of political co-optation in local politics is comparatively unexplored. Future work might examine whether co-opting local elites is an effective long-term strategy. it may not be, since once local elites have used their power to capture rents from land development, they lose their moral authority. in the short term, this may not matter to local officials as long as they can extract enough from each village to fill local coffers (as well as their own pockets). Yet there are only so many villages in which this governance strategy can be repeated, and so it may trade short-term gains for long-term problems—as has arguably occurred with social service policies or efforts to strengthen rule of law without an independent judiciary or civil society.63 these findings stand in stark contrast to a number of recent studies that have linked strong informal institutions in rural China to high levels of public goods provision.64 these findings also raise the question of whether there is a trade-off between public goods and property rights—a bargain in which villagers receive more public goods but have weaker land rights might benefit local elites. Future work on distributive politics in rural China should examine public goods alongside local development policies. outside of China, there are intriguing parallels between the role of lineage leaders and other types of local political brokers. the most obvious may be with traditional leaders such as tribal chiefs. Daron acemog˘lu, tristan reed, and James robinson find that chiefs in Sierra leone exploit their control over local civil society to control local politics and development.65 Kate Baldwin shows how national leaders cede control over land to traditional chiefs to increase electoral support among noncoethnics.66 interestingly, Baldwin also finds that voters tend to cast their ballots for political candidates endorsed by chiefs because they infer, correctly, that politicians with connections to chiefs will provide higher levels of public goods.67 this is broadly consistent with the idea that traditional kinship institutions can help buttress public goods provision, which occurs in a context of repeated interaction and relatively low stakes, but leaves open the question of what role these elite might play in large-scale land requisitions. recent work by lauren Honig finds that it is autonomous chiefs with power that historically has been independent of the state that protect their constituents from land confiscation.68 a second parallel is with other types of brokers in the developing world. there is intriguing evidence that outside of China, local brokers also trade targeted benefits in return for support for elites, who often gain much more in the bargain.69 For example, tariq thachil shows how elite parties in india use nonstate groups to supply services to the poor and in return, the poor vote for parties whose policies disproportionately benefit the elite.70 other types of brokers, such as the leaders of informal settlements,71 could play similar roles in other contexts. in general, elite capture of decentralized institutions is probably widespread. For example, Edmund Malesky, Cuong Viet nguyen, and anh tran show how in Vietnam local councils were captured by existing political elite, who diverted public resources for their own benefit.72 as a consequence, the abolition of local councils led to a surprising improvement in public service provision. My research also shows that elite capture is not inevitable. When village officials in China face competition from lineage elites who have not joined local political institutions, their behavior is constrained, and land requisitions are less likely. in this way, rival elites can play a positive role, acting as informal watchdogs and using their informal authority to encourage collective action when local officials bend the law.
researchQuestions: this work suggests that where democratic institutions are weak, strong social institutions can substitute for formal institutions like free and fair elections. in this article i argue that strong informal institutions also empower local elites who can use their influence to capture rents and confiscate property.

authorsDataDescription: the primary data for the analysis of national data come from the Chinese general Social Survey (cgss). the survey was conducted in 2005 by researchers from the Hong Kong university of Science and technology and the peoples’ university of China. the survey’s rural sample was created by stratifying among three regions (east, central, and west), then sampling seventy-five county-level units. Within each county, the survey randomly sampled four townships, and within each township, two villages. a total of 408 villages were sampled. the cgss provides high-quality data, especially in comparison to state-generated statistical yearbooks about land, which contain incomplete and likely falsified data.42 i also used the survey data to select several case studies, such as those presented above, which provided additional assurance about the survey’s accuracy. i draw on the cgss for data about village-level lineage institutions. a positive feature of the cgss is that it measures the presence of lineage groups by asking villagers directly about the salience of these groups. Some recent studies have measured the presence of lineage groups in China by coding for the presence or absence of lineage group ancestral halls or written lineage histories.

aiSummaries: **Research Question** The article examines how decentralization and informal institutions—specifically lineage groups—affect property rights in rural China (pp. 383–384) . It asks whether incorporating lineage leaders into village political institutions improves accountability or instead facilitates elite capture and weakens villagers’ land rights through increased land expropriation (pp. 384–385) . **Methods** The study employs a mixed-methods design combining qualitative case studies, a survey experiment, and national-level quantitative analysis (pp. 391–396) . Two structured village case studies illustrate causal mechanisms (pp. 391–393) . A randomized survey experiment tests whether villagers are more likely to trust endorsements of land expropriation from lineage leaders (pp. 394–396) . Regression analyses using national survey data evaluate the relationship between lineage leader incorporation and land expropriation (pp. 399–401) . **Data** The primary dataset is the 2005 Chinese General Social Survey (CGSS), which includes a rural sample of 408 villages selected through multistage stratified sampling (pp. 396–397) . The CGSS provides measures of lineage group presence, whether lineage leaders are village cadres, and whether land expropriations occurred (pp. 397–398) . Additional village-level covariates include nighttime luminosity data, geographic variables, and measures of social fragmentation (pp. 399–400) . The author also collected original survey-experimental data in a southern municipality (pp. 394–396) . **Primary Legal Sources (PRC cases, statutes, legislation, regulations, judicial interpretations, international agreements, etc.)** The article references the 1986 Land Management Law, which enabled legal transfer of land-use rights (p. 386) , and the 2003 Rural Land Contracting Law, which restricted large-scale reallocations (p. 387) . It also discusses the institutional framework of village committees and party branches governing collective land (pp. 386–387) . **Findings** Villagers are significantly more likely to trust endorsements of land expropriation from lineage leaders than from other villagers or officials (pp. 395–396; Figure 1) . National data show that when lineage leaders are incorporated into village political institutions, the likelihood of land expropriation increases by approximately 14–20 percent (pp. 400–402) . Moreover, land expropriations led by lineage leaders are associated with higher levels of petitioning and protest, suggesting more exploitative outcomes (pp. 404–406) . **Limitations and Future Research** The author acknowledges the observational nature of the national data and the possibility of unobserved time-varying factors (pp. 403–404) . Although placebo tests and robustness checks strengthen causal inference, definitive causal identification is not possible (pp. 403–404) . Future research is encouraged to explore long-term consequences of elite co-optation and the trade-offs between public goods provision and property rights protection (pp. 406–407) .

title: From Internet To Social Safety Net: The Policy Consequences Of Online Participation In China
authors: Junyan Jiang, Tianguang Meng, Qing Zhang
citation: Junyan Jiang, Tianguang Meng, Qing Zhang, From internet to social safety net: the policy consequences of online participation in China, 32 Governance, 531 (2019).
abstract: Internet-based platforms are increasingly being used by governments around the world to facilitate public engagement with citizens. However, it remains an open question whether participation through these platforms can actually enable citizens to influence policies. We address this question by studying the patterns and consequences of online participation at a major electronic petition platform in China, a country with the world's largest Internet-using population. Content analysis of over 900,000 petitions reveals that a substantial share of them concern lower-class issues and are originated from less developed rural and suburban areas. Linking variations in petition volumes to an original data set of government policy priorities, we further show that online participation led governments to place greater emphasis on social welfare policies and to increase the coverage of a key low-income assistance program. These results underscore the potential of online participation as an important mechanism to improve the quality of governance.
conclusion: As one of the most influential technological inventions of the 20th century, the Internet is seen by many as having the potential to give citizens a louder voice in the political process. However, concrete evidence on whether and how Internet-based participation can affect government policies remains limited to date. This article addresses this gap by presenting new evidence on the policy consequences of online participation from China's largest electronic petition platform. Contrary to the prevailing view that in developing countries the Internet is still a luxury technology enjoyed mainly by middle- and upper-class elites (Norris, 2001), our results suggest that when broadband infrastructure is made nearly universal, those from lower-class backgrounds will actively take advantage of new online channels to articulate their interests, and that their participation can lead local governments to place higher priority on social welfare issues in both rhetoric and actual policy implementation. Our article speaks to the literature on the relation between the Internet and governance. Existing research has found that the Internet can help strengthen government accountability (Besley & Burgess, 2002) and reduce corruption (Bailard, 2009), but may also suppress voter turnout (Campante, Durante, & Sobbrio, 2013) and increase ideological polarization (Lelkes, Sood, & Iyengar, 2017). On the issue of political inequality, more specifically, empirical findings from survey-based studies in advanced democracies (the United States in particular) tend to support the view that the Internet will reinforce, rather than alleviate, the participation and influence gaps in the offline world (Norris, 2001; Schlozman, Verba, & Brady, 2010). Our findings suggest that the conclusion drawn from the experience of developed countries may not necessarily apply to a developing context where the opportunities for conventional forms of participation are more biased and limited. We provide evidence that supports the Internet's potential to mitigate participation inequality among citizens and show that bringing in new voices into politics can substantively change governments' policy priorities. While we certainly need to be cautious to not overgeneralize findings from one platform, it is worth noting that some of the key features that made the platform effective, including operational independence, ease of access, and transparency, are not rare qualities in the cyberspace. As many other countries and regions have started to adopt similar electronic petition platforms, a promising direction of future research will be to investigate whether a similar equalizing impact also exists in other country settings. This article also contributes to a nascent but rapidly growing literature on nonelectoral responsiveness. Several recent studies have shown that in some non-Western political systems, and China in particular, inquiries and demands made by individual citizens receive replies from governments at a comparable rate as in electoral democracies (Chen et al., 2016; Distelhorst & Hou, 2017), and that individual politicians are generally attentive to citizen opinions (Meng, Pan, & Yang, 2014; Truex, 2016). So far, however, there is still limited evidence on whether, in the absence of formal electoral accountability, the preferences and demands expressed by citizens will be systematically taken into account in government policymaking—a more fundamental criterion of political responsiveness according to the original formulation of this concept (Dahl, 1998; Miller & Stokes, 1963). Our findings suggest the existence of a form of responsiveness in China that matches with its canonical definition in the democratic context. Finally, our analysis advances a more nuanced view on the relation between the Internet and the durability of the Chinese regime. While previous studies tend to portray a largely confrontational picture, focusing on either the subversive capacity of the Internet or the regime's efforts to control and manipulate the cyberspace (King, Pan, & Roberts, 2013, 2017), our findings suggest that the interaction between the two is not always zero-sum: The democratic potential of the Internet may be harnessed by the government to fulfill important governing functions. As such, the development of Internet may contribute to the regime's vitality by allowing it to improve the quality of governance without making more radical changes to its political institutions.

authorsDataDescription: To construct a consistent measure of government policies that can be compared across time and space, we apply natural language processing techniques on over 4,400 Government Work Reports (GWRs), which are comprehensive policy blueprints published annually by the Chinese government at various levels, and estimate the relative proportions of different “topics” (i.e., clusters of words) as a measure for government policy priorities. Our baseline results show that cities that receive a larger number of online petitions in a year tend to devote significantly higher proportions of government reports in the following year to a topic on social welfare, which broadly includes issues such as low-income subsidies, unemployment protection, and medical assistance. We also find that the policy effect appears to be mainly driven by petitions filed by rural residents concerning issues related to their pocketbook conditions. We subject these results to a number of robustness checks and adopt several different strategies to address the problem of endogeneity between online petition and government policy. Furthermore, leveraging data from the Minimum Living Standard Guarantee Scheme (dibao), a key low-income assistance program targeting the poor, we show that those changes in stated policy priorities are not merely empty talk, but are accompanied by substantive improvements in the coverage of the dibao program.

researchTheoriesReliedOn: The article does not present a standalone section explicitly labeled as a “theoretical framework.” Instead, the author builds on and intervenes in existing theoretical approaches to women’s political representation. First, the article engages with structural explanations of women’s underrepresentation, which attribute low female representation to macro-level constraints such as levels of economic development, women’s educational attainment, labor force participation, and broader social modernization. These structural approaches are common in comparative politics and gender studies and often treat women’s representation as a byproduct of socioeconomic conditions rather than political processes (e.g., Inglehart and Norris 2003). Second, the author draws more directly from feminist institutionalism (FI), a framework developed by scholars such as Mackay, Kenny, and Chappell. Feminist institutionalism emphasizes how formal institutions (e.g., electoral rules, quota policies) and informal institutions (e.g., gender norms, elite networks, patronage practices) interact to shape gendered political outcomes. Rather than treating institutions as neutral, FI highlights how they are embedded with power relations and gendered expectations. The author does not create an entirely new theoretical framework but extends feminist institutionalism to an authoritarian context, which is relatively underexplored in FI literature. By focusing on candidate nomination processes, party control, and quota enforcement, the article shows how informal practices within the Chinese Communist Party mediate the effects of formal gender quotas. In doing so, the author adapts FI to explain variation in women’s representation under one-party rule, demonstrating that quota effectiveness depends less on formal rules alone than on how institutions are enforced and embedded in local political contexts.

researchTheoriesReliedOn: First is a state critique theory, which posits that the goal of the Chinese leadership is to suppress dissent, and to prune human expression that finds fault with elements of the Chinese state, its policies, or its leaders. The result is to make the sum total of available public expression more favorable to those in power. Many types of state critique are included in this idea, such as poor government performance. Second is what we call the theory of collective action potential: the target of censorship is people who join together to express themselves collectively, stimulated by someone other than the government, and seem to have the potential to generate collective action."
